text: The maximal lactate steady state (MLSS) represents the highest aerobic power without metabolic energy from continuing net lactate production, and is usually sustainable for 30 to 60 minutes.
There is no evidence that training specifically at lactate threshold intensity is particularly more effective than training at other intensities.

High-performance endurance athletes dedicate 70 to 90% of their training volume to intensities below lactate threshold (light and moderate intensity) to maintain glycogen homeostasis within sustainable limits.

Three BLC-oriented training intensity domains are established: domain 1 (below threshold) for active recovery or high-volume endurance; domain 2 (threshold to MLSS) for heavy endurance training; domain 3 (MLSS to VO2max) for interval and tempo work.
Blood lactate threshold and MLSS power are highly correlated with maximum aerobic power and athletic endurance performance.
Incremental test results for blood lactate thresholds are affected by the rate of increase in power over time, meaning protocol choice influences threshold determination.
